titleStmt.title: Transcription and Translation of NRS GD55/198

respStmt.resp: Exported from Models of Authority data
respStmt.name: tei_exporter

availability.p: Status unknown. Exported for research and migration workflows.

revisionDesc.change: transcription moa=351 status=live modified=2021-04-06 12:38:54.978807+00 | translation moa=352 status=live modified=2021-04-06 12:38:52.766267+00

div.p:
Alex’ . Dei gratia Rex Scott’ . Omnibus probis hominibus totius terre sue tam clericis quam laicis Salutem . Nouerit vniuersitas uestra quod
in presentia nostra et Baronum nostrorum . ad colloquium apud Listun’ Anno gratie Millesimo . ducentesimo . tricesimo quinto .
facta est hec amicabilis compositio inter Abbatem et Monachos de Melros ex una parte . et Rogerum . Auenel ex altera . super
contentionibus motis inter eosdem . super eo scilicet quod idem . R. contra tenorem carte sue . et predecessorum suorum . posuit equicium .
aueria sua et hominum suorum . in terra ipsorum Monachorum in Eskedal’ infra diuisas contentas in carta sua . et super eo
quod dictus . R. et sui quasdam domos per dictos Monachos erectas diruerunt . et fossata et alia clausa eorundem Mona
chorum prostrauerunt . et alias eis iniurias intulerunt ut dicebant . Videlicet quod dictus . R. concessit . quod nec ipse
nec heredes sui . nec homines eorum habebunt equicium . aueria . porcos . oues . nec aliqua alia aueria domestica uel Sil
uestria infra diuisas contentas in Carta dicti . R. nisi de licentia ipsorum Monachorum . Salua dicto Rogero saluagina .
eo modo quo continetur in Carta sua . Quem modum nos et Barones nostri taliter explanauimus . Scilicet . quod idem . R. et
heredes sui habebunt tantum ceruum et ceruam . Aprum . et Apram . capreolum . et capreolam . Preterea . aerium . accipitrum .
et Sperueriorum . ita quod Monachi maliciose non impediant quin eo loco quo aeriant possint aeriare quamdiu habu
erint consuetudinem ibidem aeriandi . Neque accipitrum aut Sperueriorum capiant nidos infra dictas diuisas . Nec ar
bores in quibus aeriant uno anno secentur donec in anno proximo sequenti perpendatur si in illis arboribus uelint aeriare .
uel non . Et si tunc non aeriauerint . Licite de arboribus commodum suum facient Monachi  modis omnibus quibus uo
luerint . In aliis uero locis infra diuisas contentas in Carta dicti . R. facient logias et edificia ubicumque uoluerint pro libito
et uoluntate sua . et vtentur tenemento contento infra dictas diuisas prout melius et utilius sibi . fratribus suis . seruientibus . et
aueriis suis proficere  eis uisum fuerit . Preterea . de Latronibus et forisuiis sic determinauimus . quod non dicuntur forisuie quo
ad ius monachorum et usum eorundem . seruientium . et fratrum . et hominum suorum . ubi eunt pascendo animalia sua . uel
custodiendo . uel querendo . uel alia eorum negocia gerendo . siue de die . siue de nocte . nisi in manifesto facinore
fuerint deprehensi . Et si de nocte fuerint taliter deprehensi custodiantur usque in crastinum . qui si fuerint seruientes Mo
nachorum  ipsis statim libere tradantur ad parendum iuri ubi parere debent . De die uero capti . statim reddantur eisdem .
Dicti etiam Monachi non uenabuntur ibi cum Moetis . et cordis . nec alios ibidem ducent ad uenandum . nec pedicas
ibi ponent nisi ad capiendos lupos . Dicta siquidem Saluagina utentur dictus Rogerus . et heredes sui sicut pretaxa
tum est sine fraude et malo ingenio . absque dampno et lesione . Segetum . Sepium . Pratorum . Pecudum . Animalium . et omnium aliarum possessionum eorundem Monachorum . Testibus . A. de Moreuia . et C. de Dunblain Episcopis . W. Comi
te de Menethet . W. filio Alani Senescallo Scotie . W. Olifard Iust’ Laodonie . Henrico de Baylloll’
Dauid Mariscallo . Gaufrido clerico de Liberatione . Octauo die Aprilis . Anno Regni domini Regis vicesimo
secundo .

Alexander, by the grace of God king of the Scots, to all worthy men of the whole of his land, clerk as well as lay, sends greeting. May you all know that in our presence and of our barons at the colloquium at Liston in the Year of Grace 1235 an amicable agreement was made between the Abbot and Monks of Melrose on the one side, and Roger Avenel on the other, over the disputes instigated between them, that is, how Roger, contrary to the tenor of his charter and those of his predecessors, put a horse-stud and his own animals and men into the Monks’ land in Eskdale within the boundaries set out in his charter, and how the said Roger and his men destroyed some houses built by the said monks, and levelled their dykes and other enclosures of theirs, and brought other injuries upon them, as they said. To wit that Roger has allowed that neither he himself nor his heirs nor their men shall have a horse-stud, cattle, pigs, sheep or any other live-stock within the boundaries specified in the said Roger’s charter, unless by the monks’ permission, saving to the said Roger the beasts of the chase in the manner stated in his charter, which we and our barons have explained in this way; to wit, that Roger and his heirs shall have so many stag and hind, boar and sow, and roe-bucks and roe-does; in addition, the eyries of hawks and sparrow-hawks, provided that the monks do not maliciously cause hindrance so that they can nest in the place where they nest as long as they have the habit of nesting there; they should not remove the nests of hawks or sparrow-hawks within the said boundaries, nor should they fell the trees in which they nest in one year until it is determined whether they wish to nest in those trees in the following year or not; and if then they have not nested, let the monks make use of the trees by all means that they wish. In other places within the boundaries set out in the charter of the said Roger, they may build lodges and buildings wherever they wish, according to their pleasure and intent, and make use of the tenement contained within the said boundaries as shall seem to them best and most usefully profitable to themselves, their brethren, servants, and livestock. In addition, thus have we determined about thieves and trespassers: that they are not deemed trespassers in respect of the right of the monks and the use of the same, of their tenants, brethren, and men, whenever they go for grazing their own animals, or keeping watch, or searching, or carrying out their other business, whether by day or by night, unless they have been caught in clear wrongdoing. And if they have been discovered in such a way at night, they should be guarded until the next day; and if they are tenants of the monks, they should straightaway freely be given up in order to obey the law where they ought to obey. When captured by day, they should be returned immediately to them. Also the said monks shall not hunt there with packs of hounds and ropes, nor lead others to that place for hunting, nor set traps there except for catching wolves. Accordingly the said Roger and his heirs shall enjoy the wild game as was agreed before, without deceit and bad intention, without loss and injury of crops, hedges, meadows, livestock, animals and all the other possessions of the same monks. As witnesses, Andrew of Moray, and Clement of Dunblane, bishops; Walter, earl of Menteith; Walter son of Alan, steward of Scotland; Walter Oliphant, justiciar of Lothian; Henry de Balliol; David the Marischal; Geoffrey the clerk of the Liverance. On the eighth day of April in the twenty-second year of the reign of our lord the king [1236].
